senses.gloss: a striking together the hands as a sign that a bargain has been made, as a sign of allegiance or loyalty, and the obedience to such a contract itself

examples.text: وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَامِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَّصَلَوَاتٌ وَّمَسٰجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا ٱسْمُ ٱللهِ كَثِيرًا
examples.english: And were it not that God checks some people by others there in fact would have been demolished monastic cells and churches, and synagogues and mosques wherein the name of God is oft-mentioned.

etymology.text:
Corresponding to بَاعَ (bāʕa, “to sell”) and بَايَعَ (bāyaʕa, “to give alliance to”).

From Aramaic בֵיעֲתָא / ܒܹܝܥܬ݂ܳܐ (bēʿṯā, “egg; any egg–shaped object; oval top of an arch or other architectural element”). Doublet of بَيْض (bayḍ), which was inherited.

body.text: A striking together of the hands of two contracting parties in token of the ratification of a sale.
The oaths of allegiance and obedience

]; (Ibn-Khaldoon, in De Sacy's Chrest. Ar., 2nd ed., ii. 257; and Msb;) which the Khaleefehs exacted; (Ibn-Khaldoon;) and which El-Hajjáj appointed, including hard, or difficult, matters, relating to divorce and emancipation and fasting and the like. (Msb.)
A person occupying himself in any kind of selling
]: occurring in a trad. of Ibn-'Omar. (Mgh, TA.) And
Verily he is good in the manner of selling
(S, Mgh, Msb, K:) or, as some say,
. (Msb: [but this I think a mistake: if correct, it is a coll. gen. n.])
